title: Not one AI - a zoo
content: The word 'AI' hides many different model types, which can be grouped by what they take in and what they produce. LLMs read and write text. Vision models work with images. Speech models transcribe or generate audio. Image and video generators create media from prompts. Different model families are built for different tasks.
next: The newest species can do several of these at once.

title: Multimodal: one model, many senses
content: A multimodal model handles several formats in one system. You can show it a photo of a fridge and ask in text what to cook, and it can combine image understanding with text generation. Major frontier models are increasingly multimodal, which makes them more flexible across tasks.
next: So who actually builds these things?

title: The major providers
content: A small group of companies dominates much of the current AI news. OpenAI builds GPT models. Anthropic builds Claude. Google DeepMind builds Gemini. Meta builds Llama. Mistral AI is an important European lab. xAI builds Grok. Knowing these provider-model pairings makes current AI headlines easier to interpret.
next: One dividing line splits this list in a really important way.

title: Open weights vs closed
content: Some models are closed: you can only access them through the provider's API, and the internal weights are not released. GPT and Claude are common examples. Others publish their weights so people can download and run them on their own hardware - Meta's Llama is a well-known example. This distinction matters for control, cost, deployment flexibility, and openness.
next: With all these options - how does anyone choose?

title: The triangle: capability, speed, cost
content: Every model choice involves trade-offs. Larger models are often more capable, but also slower and more expensive per token. Smaller models are faster and cheaper, but usually less capable on hard tasks. This is why providers ship model families in different sizes: one size does not fit every use case.
next: But how do you know which model is 'best' at something?

title: Benchmarks: useful, gameable
content: Benchmarks are standardized tests - math problems, coding tasks, exam questions - used to compare models, and leaderboards rank the results. They are useful, but easy to over-trust. Models can be tuned toward popular tests, and strong benchmark scores do not guarantee the best fit for your actual use case. Benchmarks help narrow the shortlist; real testing on your own task should decide the final choice.
next: One warning before you memorize any of these names.

title: Names expire, concepts do not
content: Model names and leaderboard rankings churn every few months - whatever is 'best' today will be old news within a year. But everything else you have learned holds steady: tokens, context, prompts, tools, RAG, memory, workflows, agents describe how the pieces fit, regardless of whose model sits in the middle. Learn the map, not the traffic.
next: Which brings us to the end of the journey - and the whole stack at once.

title: You now speak AI
content: The full stack, top to bottom, now fits together: a model predicts tokens; prompts and context steer it; tools let it act; RAG supplies external knowledge; memory layers personalize behavior; APIs make access easy; workflows and agents put it to work; MCP helps connect tools through a standard. With that map, common AI buzzwords become easier to place and understand.
next: That is the whole landscape. The hype will keep changing - your map will not.
